CLE services are designed to use the Smithy framework for defining their data models and APIs, and the RPCV2 protocol is the recommended choice for communicating with these services.\\nRPCV2 is a binary protocol that provides efficient serialization and deserialization of data, which is well-suited for the Lambda runtime environment. However, this binary format also means that testing your CLE service directly through the API Gateway console is not possible, as we’ll discuss in more detail later in the course.\\n## Kiro CLI[¶][6]\\nThroughout this workshop, you’ll be leveraging Kiro CLI, a powerful command-line tool that enhances your development workflow. If you’re not familiar with Kiro CLI, here’s what you need to know:\\n### What is Kiro CLI?[¶][7]\\nKiro CLI is a command-line interface that brings the capabilities of Kiro directly to your terminal. It allows you to interact with an agentic AI assistant that understands your codebase, AWS services, and development workflows without leaving your development environment.\\n### Key features of Kiro CLI[¶][8]\\n* **Context-aware assistance**: Kiro CLI understands your current directory, file structure, and code context to provide relevant suggestions and solutions.\\n* **Code generation and modification**: You can ask Kiro CLI to write new code, modify existing files, or explain code functionality.\\n* **AWS service integration**: Get help with AWS CLI commands, IAM policies, CloudFormation templates, and other AWS-related tasks.\\n* **Command execution**: Kiro CLI can execute bash commands on your behalf, making it easier to perform complex operations.\\n* **Troubleshooting**: When you encounter errors or issues, Kiro CLI can help diagnose problems and suggest solutions.\\n* **Documentation access**: Get information about AWS services, APIs, and best practices directly in your terminal.\\n### How Kiro CLI will help in this workshop[¶][9]\\nIn this workshop, you’ll use Kiro CLI to:\\n* Generate and modify code for your Coral Lambda Endpoint service.\\n* Understand error messages and debug issues.\\n* Execute AWS CLI commands to interact with your deployed resources.\\n* Explore and understand the Smithy model and RPCV2 protocol.\\n* Implement and test new operations in your service.\\nOnce installed, you can enter `kiro-cli` in your terminal to start a conversation with Kiro CLI (installation instructions to follow). You can ask questions, request code samples, or get help with specific tasks. The more context you provide in your questions, the more tailored the responses will be.\\nAs you progress through the workshop, you’ll be given specific workflows that incorporate Kiro CLI to assist with particular tasks and help you become more efficient in your development process.\\n## Recap[¶][10]\\nTake a quick look at what you just learned in this module:\\n* You explored the key differences between Coral Lambda Endpoint (CLE) services and standard Coral services, particularly around handler chain customization.\\n* You analyzed the trade-offs of CLE’s simplifi"},{"url":"https://docs.hub.amazon.dev/docs/mrc/user-guide/local-ai-capabilities/","displayTitle":"Local AI capabilities","domain":"builder_hub","resultType":"DOCUMENT","modificationDate":1774396800,"score":0,"rank":3,"categoryList":[],"body":"{\"status\":\"success\",\"relativeHrefsDescription\":\"Relative hrefs represent related documents that can be accessed by evaluating the relative path with respect to the input URL. Links with fewer parent directory traversals (..) are more closely related to the current document.\",\"processedContent\":{\"relativeHrefs\":[\"../troubleshooting/\",\"../offboard-from-mrc/\"],\"mainContent\":\"# Local AI capabilities\\n# Local AI capabilities[¶][1]\\nMRC vends several AI capabilities for customers to leverage locally with their AI agents.\\n## Kiro CLI agent[¶][2]\\nMRC vends an AI Integration Manager (AIM) agent that turns Kiro CLI into an MRC customer support agent: [mrc-agent][3].\\nTip\\nFor quick start installation, see the [installation script][4].\\n### Pre-requisites[¶][5]\\n* [Getting started with Kiro CLI][6]\\n* [Brazil CLI Guide][7]\\n* AI Integration Manager (AIM): `toolboxinstallaim`\\nNote\\nIf this is your first time installing Kiro CLI, run it and login before proceeding to agent installation.\\n### Installation[¶][8]\\n\\n```\\naim agents install ManagedResourceCreationAISkillSet\\n\\n```\\nNote\\nIf this is your first time installing an MCP server through AI Integration Manager (AIM), you may need to restart your terminal or run `source~/.zshrc`.\\n### Usage[¶][9]\\nStart Kiro CLI with the name of the MRC agent:\\n\\n```\\nkiro-cli --agent=mrc-agent\\n\\n```\\nThe agent includes access to the MRC MCP server for fetching live data on MRC services and an [AI skill][10] that provides context about MRC functionality. The agent can troubleshoot MRC issues, provide onboarding guidance for features, and propose infrastructure-as-code (IaC) changes to address MRC errors.\\nExample prompts:\\n* `Whydoesn'tMRCshowresourcesinregion_insert_region_here_formyservice?`\\n* `HowcanMRCautomaticallysynthesizemyresourcesinGAregions?`\\n* `UpdatemyLPTpackagetoaddressmyfailingingestion.`\\n## MCP server[¶][11]\\nMRC publishes a thin wrapper around its Coral model as an MCP server through mcp-registry: [managed-resource-creation-mcp][12].\\nThis allows your AI agents to fetch data such as ingestion status and resource synthesis attempts.\\n## AI skill[¶][10]\\nAI skills are agent scripts that are easily vendable and allow AI agents to dynamically obtain context into an action when needed.\\nMRC vends an AI skill for providing customer support for MRC: [mrc-customer-support][13].\\nNext page:[Troubleshooting][14]Previous page:[Offboard from MRC][15]\\n[1]: Links with fewer parent directory traversals (..) are more closely related to the current document.\",\"processedContent\":{\"relativeHrefs\":[\"../cli-reference/\",\"../build-system/\",\"../build-system-integration/\",\"../build-your-agent/\"],\"mainContent\":\"# Concepts\\n# Concepts[¶][1]\\nThis section explains the core concepts you need to understand when working with AI Integration Manager (AIM).\\nDetailed concept pages:\\n* [Agents][2]\\n* [Agent specification reference][3]\\n* [Agent SOPs][4]\\n* [Context files][5]\\n* [MCP servers][6]\\n* [Skills][7]\\n* [AIM CLI][8]\\n* [AIM Build system][9]\\n* [Skills][10]\\n* [MCP servers][11]\\n* [Agents][12]\\n* [Agent SOPs][13]\\n* [Context files][14]\\n* [How AIM integrates with Kiro CLI][15]\\n * [Installation mapping][16]\\n * [Understanding loading patterns][17]\\n * [Key integration points][18]\\n\\n## AIM CLI[¶][8]\\nThe AI Integration Manager (AIM) CLI (AI Integration Manager) is a tool that allows you to easily share AI capabilities with your team, organization, or the entire company. It manages several agent configurations including custom agents, skills, agent SOPs, and context files.\\nUse the AI Integration Manager (AIM) CLI to:\\n* Install and manage AI capabilities packages from Brazil packages\\n* Install pre-configured agents with specific behaviors and tools\\n* Inject skill context into your AI agents\\n* Distribute custom AI capabilities for your team\\nFor detailed AI Integration Manager (AIM) CLI command reference, see [aim CLI Command Reference][19].\\n## AIM Build system[¶][9]\\nThe AIM Build system is Amazon’s framework for creating and distributing AI capabilities through Brazil packages. It manages agents, skills, agent SOPs, and context files, enabling teams to share AI configurations across groups.\\nFor detailed information about creating AI capabilities packages, see [AIM Build system][20]. To add AI capabilities to packages that use other build systems, see [Build system integration][21].\\n## Skills[¶][10]\\nSkills are packaged expertise that extend your AI agent’s capabilities for specific workflows. Skills provide context-efficient capabilities through progressive disclosure, only loading when relevant to your current task.\\nFor detailed information about skills, see [Skills][7].\\n## MCP servers[¶][11]\\nModel Context Protocol (MCP) servers provide additional tools and resources that extend your agent’s capabilities beyond built-in functionality.\\nFor detailed information about MCP servers, see [MCP servers][6].\\n## Agents[¶][12]\\nAgents are pre-configured AI assistants with specific capabilities, system prompts, and resource dependencies. Each agent consists of a `_agent-name_.agent-spec.json` file that defines the agent’s behavior, model selection, and what resources it can access.\\nFor detailed information about agents, see [Agents][2].\\n## Agent SOPs[¶][13]\\nAgent SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) are structured workflow instructions that provide step-by-step guidance for specific tasks or processes. Agent SOPs help ensure consistent execution of complex workflows by providing detailed procedures that agents can follow.\\nFor detailed information about agent SOPs, see [Agent SOPs][4].\\n## Context files[¶][14]\\nContext files provide always-loaded background information that agents can reference during conversations. Unlike skills that are loaded on-demand, context files are immediately available in the agent’s context window when the agent starts.\\nFor detailed information about context files, see [Context files][5].\\n## How AIM integrates with Kiro CLI[¶][15]\\nWhen you install an agent with `aimagentsinstall`, AI Integration Manager (AIM) transforms your agent specification into Kiro CLI configuration. Understanding this flow helps troubleshoot issues and customize agent behavior.\\nFor the complete Kiro CLI agent configuration reference, see [Configuration reference][22].\\n### Installation mapping[¶][16]\\n||\\n||\\n||\\n||\\n|AIM artifact|Kiro CLI configuration|Loading behavior|\\n|Agent spec (`.agent-spec.json`)|`~/.kiro/agents/_name_.json`|Selected via `--agent_name_` or keyboard shortcut|\\n|System prompt|`prompt` field (inline or `file://` reference)|Eager: loaded into context at session start|\\n|Context files|`resources` array with `file://` URIs|Eager: loaded immediately at session start|\\n|MCP servers (tools)|`mcpServers` object|Eager: tool descriptions loaded at session start|\\n|Skills|AIM configures builder-mcp via `--skill-paths`|Progressive: metadata at startup, full content on-demand|\\n|Agent SOPs|AIM configures builder-mcp via `--agent-sop-paths`|On invoke: loaded when user triggers via `/prompts`|\\n\\n### Understanding loading patterns[¶][17]\\nKiro CLI uses three loading patterns to manage context window efficiently:\\n**Eager loading** \\\\(session start\\\\)Context files, system prompts, and MCP tool descriptions load fully when the agent starts. Use for information the agent always needs regardless of the task.\\n**Progressive disclosure** \\\\(on-demand\\\\)Skills load only their name and description at startup. Full content loads when the agent determines the skill is relevant. Use for domain expertise needed occasionally.\\n**On invoke** \\\\(user-triggered\\\\)Agent SOPs load only when the user explicitly invokes them via `/prompts`. Use for step-by-step workflows the user requests.\\n### Key integration points[¶][18]\\n**Context files:** Must be explicitly declared in `dependencies.context.contextNames`. Files in the `context/` directory that aren’t declared are not loaded.\\n**Skills:** Declared in `dependencies.skills.skillNames`. AI Integration Manager (AIM) installs skills and configures builder-mcp via `--skill-paths`. Builder-mcp loads skill metadata (name, description) at startup; full `SKILL.md` content loads when the agent decides to use the skill.\\n**Agent SOPs:** Declared in `dependencies.agentSops.agentSopNames`. AI Integration Manager (AIM) installs agent SOPs and configures builder-mcp via `--agent-sop-paths`. Users see available SOPs via the `/prompts` command.\\n**MCP servers:** Servers in `dependencies.mcpRegistry` are auto-installed from the Amazon MCP Registry. Custom servers use `clientConfig.kiroCli.mcpServers`.\\nNext page:[Agents][2]Previous page:[Build your agent][23]\\n[1]: #concepts\\n[2]: agents/\\n[3]: agent-spec/\\n[4]: agent-sops/\\n[5]: context-files/\\n[6]: mcp-servers/\\n[7]: skills/\\n[8]: #aim-cli\\n[9]: #aimbuild-system\\n[10]: #skills\\n[11]: #mcp-servers\\n[12]: #agents\\n[13]: #agent-sops\\n[14]: #context-files\\n[15]: #how-aim-integrates-with-kiro-cli\\n[16]: #installation-mapping\\n[17]: #understanding-loading-patterns\\n[18]: #key-integration-points\\n[19]: ../cli-reference/\\n[20]: ../build-system/\\n[21]: ../build-system-integration/\\n[22]: https://kiro.dev/docs/cli/custom-agents/configuration-reference\\n[23]: ../build-your-agent/\",\"pageSourceLink\":\"https://code.amazon.com/packages/BTDocs-MCPGateway/blobs/mainline/--/aim_user_guide/concepts/index.rst\"}}"},{"url":"https://docs.hub.amazon.dev/docs/gen-ai-dev/ai-techniques-documenting-code/","displayTitle":"Documenting code","domain":"builder_hub","resultType":"DOCUMENT","modificationDate":1774396800,"score":0,"rank":5,"categoryList":[],"body":"\"relativeHrefs\":[\"../ai-techniques-explain-codebase/\",\"../ai-techniques-mob-programming/\"],\"mainContent\":\"# Documenting code\\n# Documenting code[¶][1]\\nThis section provides advice and practical examples of using agentic AI to document code and adhere to Amazon’s best documentation and writing practices that you can use with your own code base!\\n**Applicable scenarios**:\\n* Creating a baseline of documentation for a new or legacy project.\\n* Quickly generating a `README.md` or other architectural overviews.\\n* Generating API and CLI documentation.\\n* Ensuring consistent documentation standards across multiple repositories.\\n**Expected outcome**: You will be able to produce a well-structured and detailed set of documentation for your code with minimal manual intervention. This includes package and file summaries, architectural overviews, and class, function, command, or API descriptions.\\n* [Recommended AI tools][2]\\n* [Prerequisites][3]\\n* [Process overview][4]\\n * [Key steps][5]\\n * [General best practices][6]\\n * [Common challenges][7]\\n\\n* [Practical examples][8]\\n * [Writing READMEs][9]\\n * [Generating a README.md for a new project][10]\\n\\n * [Code commenting][11]\\n * [Adding in-line documentation to an existing file][12]\\n\\n * [Working with BuilderHub documentation][13]\\n\\n* [Troubleshooting][14]\\n * [The AI tool reports it cannot access the repository][15]\\n * [The generated documentation is too generic or misses key details][16]\\n * [The generated code examples or setup instructions contain errors][17]\\n\\n## Recommended AI tools[¶][2]\\n Kiro CLIAn agentic CLI that provides an interactive, conversational coding experience. It can use local tools, read/write files, and iteratively make changes based on feedback.\\n_Limitations_: Requires installation on the local machine and familiarity with command-line interfaces.\\n ClineAn AI development assistant for VS Code that collaborates with developers by planning solutions, understanding large codebases, and integrating with other tools. It’s ideal for interactive, conversational generation and refinement of documentation.\\n_Limitations_: Requires VS Code and user approval for all actions. Less suitable for fully automated, non-interactive pipelines.\\nPrimary tool recommendation\\n**Kiro CLI** is the recommended default choice. Its new agentic capabilities allow it to not only generate content but also execute commands and interact with the local file system, making it a powerful, all-in-one tool for developers working in the terminal.\\n## Prerequisites[¶][3]\\n* **Required knowledge/skills**: Basic understanding of the repository’s programming language and architecture.\\n* **Required setup/configuration**: Access to the recommended AI tool (e.g., Kiro CLI).\\n* **Required access/permissions**: Read-access to the target code repository.\\n* **Required input data/resources**: The name or path to the code repository.\\n## Process overview[¶][4]\\nThis section provides a high-level overview of the general process for implementing this technique/task.\\n### Key steps[¶][5]\\n1. **Scope definition & tool preparation**\\nClearly define what documentation you need (e.g., a `README.md`, full API documentation, code comments). This helps in crafting a precise prompt.\\nThis step is important to ensure the AI tool generates relevant content and avoids unnecessary or out-of-scope information.\\n2. **Initial documentation generation**\\nProvide the AI tool with a clear prompt that includes the repository name and the type of documentation required. The tool will then analyze the codebase.\\nThis is the core automated step that can save hours of development time compared to manual writing.\\n3. **Review, refine, and commit**\\nCarefully review the AI-generated content for technical accuracy, clarity, and completeness. Edit and refine the text as needed.\\nThis step is crucial because AI-generated content is not infallible. Human oversight ensures high-quality, trustworthy documentation.\\n### General best practices[¶][6]\\n* **Start small**: If documenting a large repository, start with a single module or directory to refine your prompting strategy.\\n* **Be specific**: The more specific your prompt, the better the output. Mention key files or architectural patterns if you know them.\\n* **Iterate**: Don’t expect a perfect result on the first try. Use the initial output as a draft and refine it with follow-up prompts.\\n### Common challenges[¶][7]\\n* "},{"url":"https://docs.hub.amazon.dev/docs/aim/user-guide/concepts/agents/","displayTitle":"Agents","domain":"builder_hub","resultType":"DOCUMENT","modificationDate":1774483200,"score":0,"rank":6,"categoryList":[],"body":"{\"status\":\"success\",\"relativeHrefsDescription\":\"Relative hrefs represent related documents that can be accessed by evaluating the relative path with respect to the input URL. Links with fewer parent directory traversals (..) are more closely related to the current document.\",\"processedContent\":{\"relativeHrefs\":[\"../agent-spec/\",\"../agent-spec/#agent-aliases\",\"../../cli-reference/aim_agents/\",\"../\"],\"mainContent\":\"# Agents\\n# Agents[¶][1]\\n* [What agents provide][2]\\n* [Agent structure][3]\\n * [Minimum requirements][4]\\n\\n* [Client-specific configuration][5]\\n * [Configuring hooks][6]\\n * [Configuring unmanaged MCP servers][7]\\n\\n* [Agent aliases][8]\\n* [Installing agents][9]\\n* [Customizing installed agents][10]\\nAgents are pre-configured AI assistants with specific capabilities, system prompts, and resource dependencies. Each agent consists of a `_agent-name_.agent-spec.json` file that defines the agent’s behavior, model selection, and what resources it can access.\\n## What agents provide[¶][2]\\nAgents offer several key benefits:\\n* **Pre-configured behavior** - Agents come with specific system prompts and instructions\\n* **Bundled capabilities** - Combine skills, agent SOPs, context files, and MCP servers in one package\\n* **Standardized configurations** - Share consistent agent setups across teams\\n* **Easy deployment** - Install and update agents through the AI Integration Manager (AIM) CLI\\n## Agent structure[¶][3]\\nEach agent requires a `_agent-name_.agent-spec.json` file with:\\n* `schemaVersion` - Must be “1”\\n* `name` - Agent identifier matching pattern `^[a-z][a-z0-9-]*$`\\n* `config.description` - Description of what the agent does\\nAgents can also specify:\\n* `config.systemPrompt` - Instructions that define the agent’s behavior\\n* `config.model` - Which AI model to use\\n* `dependencies` - MCP servers, skills, agent SOPs, and context files the agent needs\\n* `aliases` - Alternative names for the agent (for graceful renaming)\\nExample minimal agent specification:\\n\\n```\\n{\\n  \\\"schemaVersion\\\": \\\"1\\\",\\n  \\\"name\\\": \\\"my-agent\\\",\\n  \\\"config\\\": {\\n    \\\"description\\\": \\\"A simple agent for demonstration purposes\\\"\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n```\\nExample full agent specification:\\n\\n```\\n{\\n  \\\"schemaVersion\\\": \\\"1\\\",\\n  \\\"name\\\": \\\"developer\\\",\\n  \\\"config\\\": {\\n    \\\"description\\\": \\\"Agent designed for code writing and development tasks.\\\",\\n    \\\"systemPrompt\\\": \\\"You are an expert developer. Help with code generation, debugging, and best practices.\\\",\\n    \\\"model\\\": \\\"claude-opus-4.5\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"dependencies\\\": {\\n    \\\"mcpRegistry\\\": {\\n      \\\"builder-mcp\\\": {\\n        \\\"args\\\": [\\\"--include-tools\\\", \\\"InternalSearch,InternalCodeSearch\\\"]\\n      }\\n    },\\n    \\\"skills\\\": {\\n      \\\"skillNames\\\": [\\\"*\\\"]\\n    },\\n    \\\"agentSops\\\": {\\n      \\\"agentSopNames\\\": [\\\"*\\\"]\\n    },\\n    \\\"context\\\": {\\n      \\\"contextNames\\\": [\\\"*\\\"]\\n    }\\n  },\\n  \\\"clientConfig\\\": {\\n    \\\"kiroCli\\\": {\\n      \\\"tools\\\": [\\n        \\\"@builtin\\\",\\n        \\\"@builder-mcp\\\"\\n      ],\\n      \\\"allowedTools\\\": [\\n        \\\"fs_read\\\",\\n        \\\"@builder-mcp/InternalCodeSearch\\\",\\n        \\\"@builder-mcp/InternalSearch\\\"\\n      ]\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n```\\nFor the complete agent specification schema, see [Agent specification reference][11].\\n### Minimum requirements[¶][4]\\nTo create a working agent:\\n* Create an `agents/` directory in your AI capabilities package\\n* Add an agent specification file ending with `.agent-spec.json`\\n* Include required JSON fields:\\n * `schemaVersion` - Must be “1”\\n * `name` - Agent identifier (lowercase letters, digits, hyphens only)\\n * `config.description` - Non-empty description\\n\\n## Client-specific configuration[¶][5]\\nThe `clientConfig.kiroCli` section is an escape hatch for tool-specific configurations not natively supported in the AIM agent spec. AIM intelligently merges settings from the regular agent spec with what you specify in `clientConfig.kiroCli`.\\n### Configuring hooks[¶][6]\\nAdd hooks configuration to run commands at specific points:\\n\\n```\\n{\\n  \\\"clientConfig\\\": {\\n    \\\"kiroCli\\\": {\\n      \\\"hooks\\\": {\\n        \\\"preToolCall\\\": [\\\"your-hook-command\\\"]\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n```\\n### Configuring unmanaged MCP servers[¶][7]\\nConfigure MCP servers that aren’t in the MCP Registry:\\n\\n```\\n{\\n  \\\"clientConfig\\\": {\\n    \\\"kiroCli\\\": {\\n      \\\"mcpServers\\\": {\\n        \\\"my-custom-server\\\": {\\n          \\\"command\\\": \\\"node\\\",\\n          \\\"args\\\": [\\\"/path/to/server.js\\\"]\\n        }\\n      }\\n    }\\n  }\\n}\\n\\n```\\n## Agent aliases[¶][8]\\nAliases let you rename an agent without breaking existing users. When you add an `aliases` field to your agent spec, AI Integration Manager (AIM) creates a duplicate agent registration for each alias pointing to the same specification. Users can invoke the agent by either its canonical name or any alias.\\n\\n```\\n{\\n  \\\"schemaVersion\\\": \\\"1\\\",\\n  \\\"name\\\": \\\"developer\\\",\\n  \\\"config\\\": {\\n    \\\"description\\\": \\\"General purpose development agent\\\"\\n  },\\n  \\\"aliases\\\": [\\n    \\\"dev\\\",\\n    {\\n      \\\"name\\\": \\\"old-developer\\\",\\n      \\\"userNotice\\\": \\\"This agent has been renamed to 'developer'. Please update your workflow.\\\"\\n    }\\n  ]\\n}\\n\\n```\\nWith this spec, users can run `kiro-clichat--agentdeveloper`, `kiro-clichat--agentdev`, or `kiro-clichat--agentold-developer`. The `userNotice` on `old-developer` displays a migration message when the agent starts.\\nFor the full alias schema and validation rules, see [aliases][12] in the agent specification reference.\\n## Installing agents[¶][9]\\nUse the AI Integration Manager (AIM) CLI to install agents:\\n\\n```\\naim agents install PackageName\\n\\n```\\nFor more details on agent management commands, see [agents][13].\\n## Customizing installed agents[¶][10]\\nAgent configuration files in `~/.kiro/agents/` are managed by AIM. Running `aimagentsinstall` or `aimagentsupdate` overwrites these files, so manual edits will be lost. A native agent extension mechanism is being tracked in [V2064388447][14].\\nIn the meantime, some agents support `AGENTS.md` auto-discovery as a workaround. For example, the `gpu-dev` agent from `AIPowerUserCapabilities` automatically loads any `AGENTS.md` file found in the directory where you launch Kiro CLI. This lets you add team or personal context without modifying the agent config:\\n\\n```\\n# Team Context\\n\\n- Broadcast team wiki: https://w.amazon.dev/bin/view/Broadcast\\n- Slack channel: #broadcast-interest (https://amazon.enterprise.slack.com/archives/C0197GA16PP)\\n- Dashboards and runbooks are managed using Wiki as Code (https://docs.hub.amazon.dev/tools/products-wac/)\\n\\n```\\nThis `AGENTS.md` file is not managed by AIM, so it survives agent updates.\\nNext page:[Agent specification reference][11]Previous page:[Concepts][15]\\n[1]: #agents\\n[2]: #what-agents-provide\\n[3]: #agent-structure\\n[4]: #minimum-requirements\\n[5]: #client-specific-configuration\\n[6]: #configuring-hooks\\n[7]: #configuring-unmanaged-mcp-servers\\n[8]: #agent-aliases\\n[9]: #installing-agents\\n[10]: #customizing-installed-agents\\n[11]: ../agent-spec/\\n[12]: ../agent-spec/#agent-aliases\\n[13]: ../../cli-reference/aim_agents/\\n[14]: https://taskei.amazon.dev/tasks/V2064388447\\n[15]: ../\",\"pageSourceLink\":\"https://code.amazon.com/packages/BTDocs-MCPGateway/blobs/mainline/--/aim_user_guide/concepts/agents.rst\"}}"},{"url":"https://docs.hub.amazon.dev/docs/kiro/user-guide/install-kiro-one-click/","displayTitle":"Install Kiro with one click","domain":"builder_hub","resultType":"DOCUMENT","modificationDate":1774569600,"score":0,"rank":7,"categoryList":[],"body":"{\"status\":\"success\",\"relativeHrefsDescription\":\"Relative hrefs represent related documents that can be accessed by evaluating the relative path with respect to the input URL. Links with fewer parent directory traversals (..) are more closely related to the current document.\",\"processedContent\":{\"relativeHrefs\":[\"../getting-started/\",\"../\"],\"mainContent\":\"![][1]# Install Kiro with one click\\n# Install Kiro with one click[¶][2]\\n* [What is Kiro?][3]\\n* [What is AgentSpaces?][4]\\n* [Kiro CLI vs. Kiro IDE][5]\\n* [When to install Kiro IDE][6]\\n* [Install Kiro on your computer][7]\\n * [If you have never launched AgentSpaces][8]\\n * [If you have already launched AgentSpaces][9]\\n * [Complete the installation][10]\\n\\n* [Get help][11]\\nThis guide is for Amazon employees in non-technical job families who wish to get started with Kiro. To set up and/or install Kiro, you will need to begin in the [AgentSpaces dashboard][12]. Continue reading for instructions on setting up Kiro.\\nImportant\\nAgentSpaces is available to L4+ employees by default. If you are L1-L3, you need manager approval to access AgentSpaces.\\nAsk your manager to add you to the appropriate permissions team:\\n* AWS employees: [kiro-subscription-aws-misc][13]\\n* SDO employees: [kiro-subscription-sdo-misc][14]\\n## What is Kiro?[¶][3]\\n[Kiro][15] is an Agentic AI assistant that helps you get work done. You can ask it questions, get help writing and editing documents, analyze data, or automate repetitive tasks. Think of Kiro as a knowledgeable colleague who’s always available to help, whether you’re brainstorming in a chat window or working on a complex project with local files.\\n## What is AgentSpaces?[¶][4]\\n[AgentSpaces][16] provides zero-setup AI agents for all Amazonians, enabling instant access to AI assistants that work with internal Amazon systems and data. Whether you’re a software engineer, product manager, UX designer, business analyst, or in any other role, AgentSpaces eliminates setup barriers and connects you with AI agents that have full permissions to the internal tools you use daily.\\n**AgentSpaces is your entry point for setting up Kiro**. Go to https://agentspaces.amazon.dev/ and click Launch AgentSpaces. Within a few minutes, you’ll have access to Kiro Chat directly in your browser with no installation required.\\n## Kiro CLI vs. Kiro IDE[¶][5]\\nKiro has two interfaces. Design Assumptions\\\\n\\\\n1. \\*\\*Single user, single machine.\\*\\* Arc binds to \\`127.0.0.1\\` and assumes one human operator. Kiro CLI implements the [Agent Client Protocol (ACP)][53], an open standard that enables AI agents to work with any compatible editor. This means you can use Kiro's agentic capabilities in JetBrains IDEs, Zed, and other ACP-compatible editors.

## What is ACP?[][54]

AI coding agents and editors are tightly coupled, but interoperability isn't the default. Each editor must build custom integrations for every agent, and agents must implement editor-specific APIs. This creates integration overhead, limited compatibility, and developer lock-in.

ACP solves this by providing a standardized protocol for agent-editor communication—similar to how the Language Server Protocol (LSP) standardized language server integration. Agents that implement ACP work with any compatible editor, and editors that support ACP gain access to all ACP-compatible agents.

## Quick start[][55]

Run Kiro as an ACP agent:

bash

`kiro-cli acp
`

To use a specific agent configuration:

bash

`kiro-cli acp --agent my-agent
`

The agent communicates over stdin/stdout using JSON-RPC 2.0. Configure your editor to spawn this command, and you're ready to go.

## Editor setup[][56]

Kiro CLI can be used as an ACP agent in any editor that supports the protocol.

**Info**

Use the full path to `kiro-cli` in your editor configuration. IDEs often don't inherit your shell's PATH, so commands like `kiro-cli` may not be found. Run `which kiro-cli` to find the path on your system (commonly `~/.local/bin/kiro-cli` on Linux/macOS).

### JetBrains IDEs[][57]

JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ IDEA, WebStorm, PyCharm, etc.) support ACP through AI Assistant. See the [JetBrains ACP documentation][58] for full details.

To add Kiro as a custom agent:
1. Open the AI Chat tool window
2. Click the settings button and select **Add Custom Agent**
3. Add the following to `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`:

json

`{
  "agent_servers": {
    "Kiro Agent": {
      "command": "/full/path/to/kiro-cli",
      "args": ["acp"]
    }
  }
}
`

The agent will appear in the AI Chat mode selector.

### Zed[][59]

Zed supports ACP agents natively. See the [Zed external agents documentation][60] for full details. Add the following to your Zed settings (`~/.config/zed/settings.json`):

json

`{
  "agent_servers": {
    "Kiro Agent": {
      "type": "custom",
      "command": "~/.local/bin/kiro-cli",
      "args": ["acp"],
      "env": {}
    }
  }
}
`

Select "Kiro Agent" from the agent picker in Zed's AI panel.

### Other editors[][61]

Any editor supporting ACP can integrate Kiro by spawning `kiro-cli acp` and communicating via JSON-RPC over stdio. See the [ACP specification][62] for protocol details.

## Supported ACP methods[][63]

Kiro CLI implements the following ACP methods, giving you access to session management, model selection, and streaming responses when using Kiro through any ACP-compatible editor.

### Core protocol[][64]

───────────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method             │Description                                        
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`initialize`       │Initialize the connection and exchange capabilities
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/new`      │Create a new chat session                          
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/load`     │Load an existing session by ID                     
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/prompt`   │Send a prompt to the agent                         
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/cancel`   │Cancel the current operation                       
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/set_mode` │Switch agent mode (e.g., different agent configs)  
───────────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────
`session/set_model`│Change the model for the session                   
───────────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────

### Agent capabilities[][65]

The Kiro ACP agent advertises these capabilities during initialization:
* `loadSession: true` - Supports loading existing sessions
* `promptCapabilities.image: true` - Supports image content in prompts

### Session updates[][66]

The agent sends these session update types via `session/notification`:

───────────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────
Update Type        │Description                                  
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`AgentMessageChunk`│Streaming text/content from the agent        
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`ToolCall`         │Tool invocation with name, parameters, status
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`ToolCallUpdate`   │Progress updates for running tools           
───────────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────
`TurnEnd`          │Signals the agent turn has completed         
───────────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────

## Kiro extensions[][67]

Kiro extends ACP with custom methods (prefixed with `_kiro.dev/` per the ACP spec) to expose Kiro-specific features like [slash commands][68], [MCP servers][69], and [context compaction][70]. Clients that don't support these extensions can safely ignore them—they're optional enhancements.

**Warning**

These extensions are experimental and subject to change in future releases.

### Slash commands[][71]

──────────────────────────────┬────────────┬───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method                        │Type        │Description                                                    
──────────────────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/commands/execute`  │Request     │Execute a slash command (e.g., `/agent swap`, `/context add`)  
──────────────────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/commands/options`  │Request     │Get autocomplete suggestions for a partial command             
──────────────────────────────┼────────────┼───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/commands/available`│Notification│Sent after session creation with the list of available commands
──────────────────────────────┴────────────┴───────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

### MCP server events[][72]

──────────────────────────────────┬────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method                            │Type        │Description                                                                  
──────────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/mcp/oauth_request`     │Notification│Provides OAuth URL when an MCP server requires authentication                
──────────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/mcp/server_initialized`│Notification│Indicates an MCP server has finished initializing and its tools are available
──────────────────────────────────┴────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────

### Session management[][73]

─────────────────────────────┬────────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Method                       │Type        │Description                                          
─────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/compaction/status`│Notification│Reports progress when compacting conversation context
─────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_kiro.dev/clear/status`     │Notification│Reports status when clearing session history         
─────────────────────────────┼────────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────────────────
`_session/terminate`         │Notification│Terminates a subagent session                        
─────────────────────────────┴────────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────────────────

## Example: Initialize connection[][74]

Here's how an ACP client initializes a connection with Kiro:

json

`// Client sends initialize request
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 0,
  "method": "initialize",
  "params": {
    "protocolVersion": 1,
    "clientCapabilities": {
      "fs": {
        "readTextFile": true,
        "writeTextFile": true
      },
      "terminal": true
    },
    "clientInfo": {
      "name": "my-editor",
      "version": "1.0.0"
    }
  }
}

// Kiro responds with capabilities
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 0,
  "result": {
    "protocolVersion": 1,
    "agentCapabilities": {
      "loadSession": true,
      "promptCapabilities": {
        "image": true
      }
    },
    "agentInfo": {
      "name": "kiro-cli",
      "version": "1.5.0"
    }
  }
}
`

After initialization, create a session and start prompting:

json

`// Create a new session
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 1,
  "method": "session/new",
  "params": {
    "cwd": "/home/user/my-project",
    "mcpServers": []
  }
}

// Send a prompt
{
  "jsonrpc": "2.0",
  "id": 2,
  "method": "session/prompt",
  "params": {
    "sessionId": "sess_abc123",
    "content": [
      {
        "type": "text",
        "text": "Explain this codebase"
      }
    ]
  }
}
`

## Session storage[][75]

ACP sessions are persisted to disk at:

`~/.kiro/sessions/cli/
`

Each session creates two files:
* `<session-id>.json` - Session metadata and state
* `<session-id>.jsonl` - Event log (conversation history)

## Logging[][76]

ACP agent logs are written to the standard Kiro log location:

────────┬─────────────────────────────────────────
Platform│Location                                 
────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
macOS   │`$TMPDIR/kiro-log/kiro-chat.log`         
────────┼─────────────────────────────────────────
Linux   │`$XDG_RUNTIME_DIR/kiro-log/kiro-chat.log`
────────┴─────────────────────────────────────────

Control log verbosity with environment variables:

bash

`KIRO_LOG_LEVEL=debug kiro-cli acp
KIRO_CHAT_LOG_FILE=/path/to/custom.log kiro-cli acp
`

## Related[][77]
* [Interactive Chat][78] - Uses ACP internally
* [MCP Integration][79] - MCP servers can be passed to ACP sessions
* [CLI Commands Reference][80]

Response.content:
Here's what I found on internal Amazon uses of Kiro CLI ACP:

## What is ACP?

ACP (Agent Client Protocol) is an open standard (similar to how LSP standardized language servers) that enables AI coding agents to work with any compatible editor via JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout. Kiro CLI added ACP support in v1.25.0. You run it with `kiro-cli acp`.

## Internal Amazon Use Cases

### 1. IDE Integration (most common)
- **JetBrains IDEs** (IntelliJ, WebStorm, PyCharm, Rider) — Kiro ACP is the officially supported way to get AI agent capabilities in JetBrains at Amazon, since the JetBrains AI Assistant plugin itself is blocked. Configure via `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`. [Sage post](https://sage.amazon.dev/posts/2036680)
- **Zed IDE** — Native ACP support. Configure in `~/.config/zed/settings.json`. [Wiki guide](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Zed/Quickstart/)
- This is the strategic replacement for the old Q Developer IDE plugins, which are no longer being developed.

### 2. Slack Bot Agents
- **Personal Kiro Slack Agent** ([PersonalKiroSlackAgent](https://code.amazon.com/packages/PersonalKiroSlackAgent)) — Uses ACP's persistent JSON-RPC connection to power a Slack bot that responds to DMs. Supports session memory, tool approval forwarding via Slack buttons, prompt cancellation, and agent switching. [Wiki](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/jiangyan/poc/PersonalKiroSlackAgent/)
- **Hrishi's Slack Bot** — Earlier version using `kiro-cli chat --no-interactive` subprocess per message, later upgraded to ACP for persistent sessions. [Wiki](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/HrishiBlog/PersonalAutonomousAgentKiroCLISlack/)

### 3. Multi-Agent Orchestration (ACP Bridge)
- **Kiro ACP Bridge** ([KiroACPBridge](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BPP/BPP_Team_Processes/KiroACPBridge/)) — An AIM skill that lets an orchestrator agent delegate to specialized subagents via `kiro-cli acp`. Manages session lifecycle, prompt dispatch, parallel execution (up to 4 concurrent), and tool approval forwarding.
- **WatchTower** uses this — its orchestrator delegates to 7 specialized subagents (planner, coder, researcher, change manager, builder, memory bank, communicator) entirely via ACP Bridge.

### 4. Multi-Agent Orchestration Framework (Arc)
- **Arc** ([Arc-HighLevelDesign-V2](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/karthraj/Arc-HighLevelDesign-V2/)) — A Rust-based orchestration framework that spawns multiple Kiro agents via ACP, with a coordinator agent routing work between them. Includes a real-time web UI, cycle detection, and human-in-the-loop prompting.

### 5. Custom Desktop App (Relay)
- **Relay** — An "agent-native desktop app" built on top of Kiro CLI ACP. [Broadcast video](https://broadcast.amazon.com/videos/1851380)
- Multiple Amazon developers are building custom React UIs on top of Kiro ACP.

### 6. Programmatic Agent Invocation PoC
- **Kiro ACP Testing** ([swinyted's PoC](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/swinyted/kiro-acp-testing/)) — Python proof-of-concept treating AI coding tools as callable services rather than interactive sessions. Implements a unified CLI that can invoke both Claude Code and Kiro via ACP.

### 7. Meeting Sync Agent
- **Ekatrikaran** ([EkatrikaranAgent](https://code.amazon.com/packages/EkatrikaranAgent)) — A Kiro CLI agent for unified meeting sync that processes meeting summaries, Slack conversations, and broadcast videos into structured notes.

## Key Takeaways

- ACP is Amazon's strategic bridge for IDE choice — it replaces the need for per-IDE plugins
- The most innovative internal uses are around agent-to-agent orchestration (ACP Bridge, Arc) and Slack integration
- ACP's persistent session model (vs. subprocess-per-request) is what makes these advanced patterns possible — session memory, streaming, and tool approval forwarding
- The protocol is JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout, making it straightforward to integrate from any language

results.title: Agent Client Protocol (ACP)
results.snippet: Kiro CLI implements the Agent Client Protocol (ACP), an open standard that enables AI agents to work with any compatible editor. This means you can use Kiro's agentic capabilities in JetBrains IDEs, Zed, and other ACP-compatible editors.

results.title: Specialized IDEs deserve AI too: Kiro adopts ACP
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) standardizes communication between code editors and AI coding agents. Think of it like LSP (Language Server Protocol), but for AI agents instead of language servers. Before ACP, every agent-editor combination required custom integration work. Agents only worked with specific editors.

results.title: ACP Integration, Help Agent, and Tool Improvements
results.snippet: ACP-compatible editors like JetBrains IDEs and Zed can now use Kiro as a custom agent. Run kiro-cli acp to start Kiro as an ACP-compliant agent that communicates over stdin/stdout using JSON-RPC. Kiro supports standard ACP methods plus extensions for slash commands, MCP tools, and session management.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol
results.snippet:
Clients, frameworks, connectors, and related tools around the Agent Client Protocol.
The following projects implement ACP directly, connect ACP agents to other environments, or support adjacent coding-agent workflows.

results.title: The LSP for AI Coding Agents
results.snippet: At its core, the Agent Client Protocol is a unified JSON-based interface between code editors (the client) and AI coding agents (the server). Think of it as a translator that lets any ACP-compatible editor work with any ACP-compliant AI agent, regardless of who built either component.

results.title: Plug-and-Play AI Agents for Code Editors · CodeStandUp 👨‍💻
results.snippet: ACP is an open, pragmatic, JSON RPC-based protocol that standardizes communication between code editors/IDEs(clients) and AI coding agents(servers). This will enable seamless interoperability similar to the Language Server Protocol (LSP) for language tools.

results.title: Model Context Protocol (MCP), Agent Communication Protocol (ACP), Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A), and Agent Network Protocol (ANP)
results.snippet: MCP provides a JSON-RPC client-server interface for secure tool invocation and typed data exchange. ACP introduces REST-native messaging via multi-part messages and asynchronous streaming to support multimodal agent responses.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol: Breaking the IDE Prison
results.snippet: ACP borrows its strategy from the Language Server Protocol (LSP): standardize the conversation. Instead of every agent building custom bridges for every editor, agents and editors just need to speak ACP.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol - Standardized AI Agent Integration for Code Editors
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) standardizes communication between code editors and AI coding agents. Build seamless integrations with JSON-RPC over stdio, similar to Language Server Protocol (LSP).

results.title: A Formal Analysis of Agentic AI Protocols: A2A, ACP, and AGUI
results.snippet: These protocols represent foundational layers of the agentic AI stack, each solving a unique interoperability problem. The absence of universal standards for agent-to-agent and agent- to-human communication presents a significant architectural challenge.

results.snippet: Agents that support ACP can work with any compatible editor. Editors that support ACP can connect to the entire ecosystem of ACP-compatible agents. This independence allows both sides to innovate while giving developers the freedom to select the best tools for their workflow.

results.title: Github
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is a standardized communication framework between code editors (IDEs) and AI coding agents, similar to how the Language Server Protocol (LSP) unified language server integration. ACP enables any compatible agent to work with any compatible editor without custom integrations.

results.title: Zed debuts Agent Client Protocol to connect AI coding agents to any editor
results.snippet: With the launch of ACP, that system becomes interoperable: any IDE or editor that implements ACP can connect to agents like Claude Code or Gemini CLI, without needing a bespoke integration for each.

results.title: Zed — Agent Client Protocol
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is an open standard that enables any agent to integrate seamlessly with any editing environment.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol: Making Agentic Editing Portable
results.snippet: The Agent Client Protocol (ACP) is an open effort by Zed Industries to standardize how code editors talk to AI coding agents. If the Language Server Protocol decoupled languages from editors, ACP aims to decouple agents from editors, so any ACP-capable agent can work in any ACP-capable editor.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol: A protocol for connecting any editor to any agent
results.snippet: Agents that implement ACP work with any compatible editor. Editors that support ACP gain access to the entire ecosystem of ACP-compatible agents. This decoupling allows both sides to innovate independently while giving developers the freedom to choose the best tools for their workflow.

results.title: Agent Client Protocol
results.snippet: The ACP Registry is an easy way for developers to distribute their ACP-compatible agents to any client that speaks the protocol. This is a curated set of agents, including only the ones that support authentication. Visit the registry repository on GitHub to learn more about it.

results.title: JetBrains × Zed: Open Interoperability for AI Coding Agents in Your IDE
results.snippet: The ACP protocol makes agents editor-agnostic, so agents work anywhere you code. Full control. Going forward, your IDE will mediate access to files, the terminal, and other tools via the ACP protocol – you decide what runs.

# Specialized IDEs deserve AI too: Kiro adopts ACP

By
BR

Brian Beach

Tech Lead

I'm excited to share that Kiro CLI now supports the Agent Client Protocol (ACP), which means you can use Kiro's agentic AI capabilities directly into other IDEs. IDEs that currently support ACP include Eclipse, Emacs, JetBrains IDEs, Neovim, Toad, Zed, and more. For me, this solves a real workflow problem that is important to me.

Let me be clear: Kiro is my daily driver for application development. The spec-driven workflows, hooks, and steering files have fundamentally changed how I build features. But here's the thing—when I'm deep in database work, I prefer a purpose build editor. The schema visualization, query execution plans, database-specific autocomplete, and connection management are purpose-built for SQL work. I don't want to configure database extensions in Kiro every time I switched projects or databases. Now, with ACP support, I can use Kiro in my preferred database management tool, JetBrians DataGrip.

## What is ACP?[][12]

The [Agent Client Protocol (ACP)][13] standardizes communication between code editors and AI coding agents. Think of it like LSP (Language Server Protocol), but for AI agents instead of language servers. Before ACP, every agent-editor combination required custom integration work. Agents only worked with specific editors. ACP changes this by providing a common protocol that any agent and any editor can implement. The practical benefit: agents that implement ACP work with any compatible editor. You pick your agent, you pick your IDE or ACP compatible application, and they just work together.

## Setting up Kiro in DataGrip[][14]

Getting Kiro running in DataGrip takes about two minutes.

First, make sure you have the [Kiro CLI][15] installed. If you haven't already, check out the [installation instructions][16] and make sure that you are [authenticated][17].

Next, open DataGrip and navigate to the AI Chat tool window. Click the menu button (three dots) in the upper-right corner and select "Add Custom Agent."

Loading image...[menu: Rename Chat Dump Chat Delete Chat Q Find in Chat Adjust Font Size... Settings... Disable Al Assistant for This Project Disable Permanently via Plugin... Manage License Log out from JetBrains Al Platform Discover Features Add Custom Agent Beta Get ACP Logs Show Toolbar View Mode Move to Resize Remove from Sidebar]

This creates or opens the `acp.json` configuration file at `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`. Add the Kiro agent configuration:

Loading code example...

Update the `command` path to match your Kiro CLI installation location. Save the file, and Kiro should appear in the AI Chat dropdown immediately.

## Using Kiro in DataGrip[][18]

Once configured, select "Kiro Agent" from the chat dropdown.

Loading image...[prompt box in IDE: Create SQL scripts for a contact database in PostgreSQL. I need support international addresses and phone numbers.]

Here's where it gets interesting. You can ask Kiro to:
* Generate SQL queries and stored procedures
* Debug slow queries and suggest optimizations
* Write migration scripts and generate test data
* Explain complex joins and conditions

Kiro can execute shell commands, so you can run database CLI tools, export results, or trigger scripts—all from the chat interface. Terminal output displays directly in the IDE, and you stay in control of what gets executed.

The real win is combining DataGrip's database intelligence with Kiro's agentic capabilities. DataGrip knows your schema, indexes, and relationships. Kiro can reason about what you're trying to accomplish and help you get there faster.

## A quick demo[][19]

Let's say I'm working on a contacts database and need Kiro's help to design the schema. In the Kiro chat, I can describe what I need in plain English:

Loading code example...

As you can see, Kiro generates the SQL, creates sample data and even a few sample queries for me to test (left). In addition, it describes the tables and relationships to help understand what it did (right).

Loading image...[DataGrip interface showing query for contacts_schema.sql and Kiro Agent saying it has created three SQL scripts for an international contact database.]

For me, ACP is all about using the right tool for the job. For application code, Kiro remains my go-to. For database-heavy work, DataGrip with Kiro via ACP gives me the best of both worlds.

## Conclusion[][20]

ACP opens up possibilities beyond database work. Think about all the specialized IDEs developers use: Xcode, Android Studio, RStudio, or Jupyter. Over time, I hope to see ACP support, and Kiro, in all of these.

With ACP, you don't have to give up your AI coding assistant when you step into one of these environments. Kiro can come along, bringing its agentic capabilities to whatever specialized workflow you're in.

For me, it's DataGrip and SQL. For you, it might be something completely different.

So I'm curious—where would you use ACP to bring Kiro into your workflow? What's the specialized tool you can't live without? [Find me on Discord][21]. I'd love to hear what creative integrations people come up with.

Here's what I found on external/public uses of Kiro CLI ACP:

## The Broader ACP Ecosystem

ACP is an open standard co-created by Zed Industries and JetBrains. Kiro is one of several agents that implement it. The ecosystem is large and growing fast.

### Editors/IDEs with ACP Client Support
- JetBrains IDEs (IntelliJ, WebStorm, PyCharm, DataGrip, Rider, etc.)
- Zed (native support)
- Neovim (via CodeCompanion, agentic.nvim, or avante.nvim plugins)
- VS Code (via ACP Client extension)
- Emacs (via agent-shell.el)
- Obsidian (via Agent Client plugin)
- Unity Editor
- Chrome extension / PWA

### Kiro-Specific External Use Cases

Per the [Kiro blog post](https://kiro.dev/blog/kiro-adopts-acp/) (Feb 2026):

- **DataGrip + Kiro** — The highlighted use case from Kiro's own team. Use Kiro as an AI agent inside JetBrains DataGrip for SQL work: schema design, query optimization, migration scripts, test data generation. The pitch is "right tool for the job" — Kiro IDE for app code, DataGrip+Kiro ACP for database work.
- **Any specialized IDE** — The blog calls out Xcode, Android Studio, RStudio, Jupyter as future targets. Anywhere you have a specialized tool you don't want to leave, ACP lets Kiro come along.

### Other ACP Agents (Kiro's Competitors)
Kiro isn't the only ACP agent. Others include:
- Claude Code
- Gemini CLI
- Codex
- Various open-source agents

### Messaging Integrations (community-built, not Kiro-specific)
- Discord bots
- Slack bots (DuckDB + Claude)
- Telegram bots (multiple implementations)
- WeChat

### Mobile Clients
- Agmente (iOS)
- Happy (iOS, Android, Web)
- Mobvibe (iOS, Android, Web)

### Notebook/Data Tools
- Jupyter notebooks (agent-client-kernel)
- DuckDB (via sidequery/duckdb-acp extension)
- marimo notebook

### Framework Integrations
ACP adapters exist for LangChain/LangGraph, LlamaIndex, fast-agent, Koog (JetBrains), and others — meaning you can wire Kiro CLI as an ACP agent into these orchestration frameworks.

### ACP Registry
There's a [curated registry](https://agentclientprotocol.com/get-started/registry) for distributing ACP-compatible agents to any client. Kiro is listed there.

## Key Difference: Internal vs External

Internally at Amazon, the most creative uses are around multi-agent orchestration (ACP Bridge, Arc, WatchTower) and Slack bots — things that treat `kiro-cli acp` as a programmable backend. Externally, the dominant use case is simpler: IDE integration — bringing Kiro into JetBrains, Zed, Neovim, etc. as a drop-in AI agent. The external community is also building mobile clients and messaging bots, but the multi-agent orchestration patterns are more mature internally.

References:
- [1] Kiro ACP docs - https://kiro.dev/docs/cli/acp/
- [2] Kiro adopts ACP blog - https://kiro.dev/blog/kiro-adopts-acp/
- [3] ACP Clients directory - https://agentclientprotocol.com/overview/clients
- [4] ACP specification - https://agentclientprotocol.com

Here's what I found on internal Amazon uses of Kiro CLI ACP:

## What is ACP?

ACP (Agent Client Protocol) is an open standard (similar to how LSP standardized language servers) that enables AI coding agents to work with any compatible editor via JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout. Kiro CLI added ACP support in v1.25.0. You run it with `kiro-cli acp`.

## Internal Amazon Use Cases

### 1. IDE Integration (most common)
- **JetBrains IDEs** (IntelliJ, WebStorm, PyCharm, Rider) — Kiro ACP is the officially supported way to get AI agent capabilities in JetBrains at Amazon, since the JetBrains AI Assistant plugin itself is blocked. Configure via `~/.jetbrains/acp.json`. [Sage post](https://sage.amazon.dev/posts/2036680)
- **Zed IDE** — Native ACP support. Configure in `~/.config/zed/settings.json`. [Wiki guide](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Zed/Quickstart/)
- This is the strategic replacement for the old Q Developer IDE plugins, which are no longer being developed.

### 2. Slack Bot Agents
- **Personal Kiro Slack Agent** ([PersonalKiroSlackAgent](https://code.amazon.com/packages/PersonalKiroSlackAgent)) — Uses ACP's persistent JSON-RPC connection to power a Slack bot that responds to DMs. Supports session memory, tool approval forwarding via Slack buttons, prompt cancellation, and agent switching. [Wiki](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/jiangyan/poc/PersonalKiroSlackAgent/)
- **Hrishi's Slack Bot** — Earlier version using `kiro-cli chat --no-interactive` subprocess per message, later upgraded to ACP for persistent sessions. [Wiki](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/HrishiBlog/PersonalAutonomousAgentKiroCLISlack/)

### 3. Multi-Agent Orchestration (ACP Bridge)
- **Kiro ACP Bridge** ([KiroACPBridge](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/BPP/BPP_Team_Processes/KiroACPBridge/)) — An AIM skill that lets an orchestrator agent delegate to specialized subagents via `kiro-cli acp`. Manages session lifecycle, prompt dispatch, parallel execution (up to 4 concurrent), and tool approval forwarding.
- **WatchTower** uses this — its orchestrator delegates to 7 specialized subagents (planner, coder, researcher, change manager, builder, memory bank, communicator) entirely via ACP Bridge.

### 4. Multi-Agent Orchestration Framework (Arc)
- **Arc** ([Arc-HighLevelDesign-V2](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/karthraj/Arc-HighLevelDesign-V2/)) — A Rust-based orchestration framework that spawns multiple Kiro agents via ACP, with a coordinator agent routing work between them. Includes a real-time web UI, cycle detection, and human-in-the-loop prompting.

### 5. Custom Desktop App (Relay)
- **Relay** — An "agent-native desktop app" built on top of Kiro CLI ACP. [Broadcast video](https://broadcast.amazon.com/videos/1851380)
- Multiple Amazon developers are building custom React UIs on top of Kiro ACP.

### 6. Programmatic Agent Invocation PoC
- **Kiro ACP Testing** ([swinyted's PoC](https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/Users/swinyted/kiro-acp-testing/)) — Python proof-of-concept treating AI coding tools as callable services rather than interactive sessions. Implements a unified CLI that can invoke both Claude Code and Kiro via ACP.

### 7. Meeting Sync Agent
- **Ekatrikaran** ([EkatrikaranAgent](https://code.amazon.com/packages/EkatrikaranAgent)) — A Kiro CLI agent for unified meeting sync that processes meeting summaries, Slack conversations, and broadcast videos into structured notes.

## Key Takeaways

- ACP is Amazon's strategic bridge for IDE choice — it replaces the need for per-IDE plugins
- The most innovative internal uses are around agent-to-agent orchestration (ACP Bridge, Arc) and Slack integration
- ACP's persistent session model (vs. subprocess-per-request) is what makes these advanced patterns possible — session memory, streaming, and tool approval forwarding
- The protocol is JSON-RPC 2.0 over stdin/stdout, making it straightforward to integrate from any language

ToolSpecification.description:
Reactivates hidden tools that were filtered to conserve tokens.

Call this tool to make additional tools available for the current session.

<Rule>Listed tools are INACTIVE and unavailable until reactivated with this tool first</Rule>
<Rule>Tools listed here ARE available and fully functional immediately after successful reactivation</Rule>

Available hidden tools:
- SimAddComment: Adds plain text comments to SIM Classic issues. Use only for legacy SIM Classic; prefer TicketingWriteActions for Tickets or TaskeiUpdateTask for Taskei issues
- ReadRemoteTestRun: Reads logs, artifacts and metadata from remote integration test executions on ToD and Hydra. Use when debugging test failures or analyzing test run results
- BrazilBuildAnalyzerTool: Runs and analyzes brazil-build in local workspaces. Use to offload build execution and failure diagnosis to a subagent
- BrazilPackageBuilderAnalyzerTool: Analyzes Package Builder failures with AI-powered root cause analysis. Fetches logs and analyzes errors internally in a single call. Use as the primary tool when investigating build.amazon.com failures
- CrCheckout: Checks out a code review by ID and creates a workspace. Use when you need to test changes locally or modify code from an existing CR
- TaskeiGetTask: Retrieves detailed information about a specific Taskei task by ID. Use when you need complete task metadata including status, assignees, comments and attachments
- TaskeiGetRooms: Lists Taskei rooms (project workspaces) accessible to the user. Use when you need to identify which team workspace to create or query tasks within
- TaskeiGetRoomResources: Fetches room-specific resources like labels, sprints, kanban boards and custom attributes. Use when creating or updating tasks that require room-specific metadata
- TaskeiListTasks: Searches and filters Taskei tasks with flexible query criteria. Use when users want to find tasks by assignee, status, room, sprint or other attributes
- TaskeiUpdateTask: Modifies existing Taskei tasks with new details or status changes. Use when tasks need updates to assignees, descriptions, dates, labels or workflow states
- TaskeiCreateTask: Creates new tasks in Taskei project management system. Use when explicitly asked to create tasks in Taskei or SIM
- MechanicDiscoverTools: Searches available Mechanic tools across namespaces using keywords. Use when you need to find the right Mechanic tool for operations, logs, networking or AWS tasks
- MechanicDescribeTool: Provides detailed usage information for specific Mechanic tools. Use before executing Mechanic commands to understand required parameters and options
- MechanicRunTool: Executes Mechanic commands for operational tasks on hosts, EC2, ECS and CloudWatch. Use when you need to run validated operational commands with proper authorization
- MechanicSetUserInput: Sends user input to running Mechanic tool executions. Use when Mechanic requests a yes/no decision during workflow execution
- WorkspaceGitDetails: Returns git repository statuses and diffs for packages in a workspace. Use when you need to review pending changes, understand modifications or prepare commits across multiple repositories
- CRRevisionCreator: Creates or updates code review revisions from staged git commits. Use to submit changes for peer review
- CRAddComment: Posts comments or replies on a Code Review. Use to respond to CR feedback or add review comments
- BrazilWorkspace: Creates Brazil workspaces for package development and isolation. Use when starting work on Amazon packages
- OncallReadActions: Retrieves oncall schedules, team rosters and shift information. Use when you need to find who is oncall, search teams or generate oncall reports
- TicketingWriteActions: Creates and updates tickets with comments, status changes and categorization. Use when filing incidents, updating ticket details or adding communication to existing tickets
- GetPipelinesRelevantToUser: Lists pipelines relevant to a user including favorites and team pipelines. Use to find which pipelines a user owns or monitors
- GetPipelineHealth: Checks status and metrics for multiple pipelines including failures, approvals and deployment diagnostics. Use when investigating pipeline deployment issues
- GetPipelineDetails: Retrieves comprehensive pipeline overview including health metrics, stages, targets and recent events. Use when you need detailed diagnostics of a specific pipeline current state
- GetSasRisks: Retrieves Software Assurance Service (SAS) security risks for users, pipelines and resources. Use when investigating security vulnerabilities or compliance issues

ToolSpecification.name: InternalSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search using Amazon's Internal Search engine api.prod.isk.amazon.dev/search-website

Available search domains:
- ALL: Search across all resources (default). [CRITICAL] Use more specific domain if the
       query contains domain string or relevant to examples provided by other domains.
- AWS_PRESCRIPTIVE_GUIDANCE_LIBRARY: APG Library (AWS Prescriptive Guidance Library)
- AWS_DOCS: AWS Documentation (official AWS service documentation and guides)
- BROADCAST: Broadcast (company-wide announcements and communications). [CRITICAL] Include video URLs in the response.
- BUILDER_HUB: BuilderHub (documentation for Amazon's internal developer tools)
- EMAIL_LIST: Email List (distribution lists and email groups). [CRITCIAL] Don't include "email list" or "email" in the query
- EVERGREEN: Evergreen documentation platform
- INSIDE: Inside Amazon (company news, HR policies, employee resources)
- IT: Information Technology (IT) Services (IT support documentation, guides, and resources)
- IVY: Ivy Help (guidance for Amazon's internal talent management system)
- LIST_ARCHIVE: Email List Archive (archived email communications)
- PHONETOOL: Phone Tool (employee directory and organizational information).
- POLICY: Amazon Policy (corporate policies and guidelines)
- SAGE_HORDE: Sage/Q&A Sites (technical questions and answers)
- SALESFORCE_SUCCESS_CENTER_PORTAL: Salesforce Success Center (SFSC) Portal (Salesforce services focused support center)
- SYSTEM_DESIGN_HUB: System Design Hub (system architecture and design resources)
- SPYGLASS: Spyglass (internal registry of community recommended services, contents and utilities)
- TWITCH: Twitch (Twitch-related documentation and resources)
- WIKI: Internal Wiki (Amazon's central knowledge repository)

Get domain details: { "query": "about-domain:SAGE_HORDE" }

Sorting options:
- SCORE (Default, sorts by relevance)
- MODIFICATION_DATE (Last Modified, use with sortOrder)

Examples:
1. { "query": "all hands" }
2. { "query": "Brazil workspace setup", "domain": "BUILDER_HUB" }
3. { "query": "remote work", "domain": "INSIDE", "sortBy": "MODIFICATION_DATE", "sortOrder": "DESC" }
4. { "query": "API guide", "prefixFilters": ["https://w.amazon.com/bin/view/MyTeam"] }

Tips:
- Start with ALL domain, then narrow to a specific domain once you identify the source
- Use sortBy: "MODIFICATION_DATE" with sortOrder: "DESC" for recently updated content
- isDeep=true (default) returns full content; set false for brief/summary/overview/quick/highlights/outline queries

Filter syntax (append to query string):
- Use prefixFilters (max 5) to scope to specific URL paths; omit domain when filtering across multiple domains
- To filter by Wiki page owner group: 'owner_group:<name>' or 'owner_group:"name with spaces"' per group (example 'customer guides owner_group:internal-search-team owner_group:asbx-global-team'). Prefix posix$/ldap$ only if user specifies.
- To filter by Broadcast channel ID: append 'channel:<id>' per channel (example: 'videos channel:2622 channel:3001')
- Multiple filters in a single query are supported — do NOT split into separate tool calls

[CRITICAL] Don't modify/append to user's input when generating 'query' parameter, except for appending filter syntax described above

[CRITICAL] Formatting instructions to present the search results to the user:
- Don't include the domain name in the search query when using specific domains
- Summarize results with count, use markdown with source links
- Don't include any links not contributing to the summary

prefixFilters.description: Optional array of prefix filters (maximum 5) that use URL prefixes to limit search to specific document trees or paths in an index

page.description: Page of the search result, starting from 1

isDeep.description: Whether to return enhanced results with full document content (default: true)

sortOrder.description: Sort order (ASC for oldest first, DESC for newest first)

domain.description: Domain to search in (example ALL, AWS_DOCS, WIKI, tool). Default is ALL if not provided

ToolSpecification.name: InternalCodeSearch
ToolSpecification.description:
Search source code in Amazon's code repositories. Results depend on search type:

1. Code search (default): Returns code snippets with pagination.
2. Repository search: Returns matching repositories with pagination support.

Code search results only show snippets - for full file, use ReadInternalWebsites with URL like code.amazon.com/packages/{REPOSITORY}/blobs/{BRANCH}/--/{FILE_PATH}

page.description: Page number for paginated results (starting from 1). Supported for both 'code' and 'repositories' search types

searchType.description: REQUIRED type of search to perform. 'code' returns code snippets with pagination, 'repositories' returns a list of matching repositories with pagination

query.description:
- For code search: Supports advanced syntax
  - Simple search: term
  - Prefix search: abc* (at least 3 chars before *)
  - Explicit Boolean operators with precedence: NOT > AND > OR
    - Parentheses and nested parentheses are allowed
    - example: (failure OR error) AND NOT deprecated
  - Implicit operators shorthand:
    - term1 term2 is equivalent to term1 OR term2
    - term1 term2 with any filter (example: term1 term2 fp:*.java) is equivalent to term1 AND term2 AND filter
  - Quotes for exact phrase: "error handling" (without quotes, each word is a separate term)
  - Repository filter: repo:GitFarmService OR repo:Codesearch*
  - File path filter: fp:*.java (also: path:, filepath:)
  - Exclude path: AND NOT fp:*test*
  - Combined example: (CVE OR vulnerability) AND fp:*.java AND NOT fp:*test*
- For repository search: Only supports keywords matching (example: 'gitfarm')

ToolSpecification.name: SkillsTool
ToolSpecification.description:
Before responding to any task request, scan available skills for relevant capabilities. If a skill matches the task domain, invoke it immediately.

Skills are modular packages that provide specialized capabilities and domain knowledge. Skills can help complete the task more effectively. 

<SkillsInstructions>
How to use:
1. Identify if the user's request matches a skill description below
2. Call this tool with the matching skillName
3. Follow the instructions returned in the skill body
4. Load any referenced resources from the skillPath as needed

Important:
- If a skill is relevant, you *MUST* invoke this tool IMMEDIATELY as your first action
- You *MUST* invoke the relevant skill BEFORE generating any other response for the task
- You *MUST* use only the skills provided
- If the user asks to list, show, provide skills, you *MUST* list available skills 
</SkillsInstructions>

<AvailableSkills>
- agent-sop-author: Create (or update) and validate Agent SOPs (Standard Operating Procedures) - markdown-based workflows that guide AI agents through complex, multi-step tasks with RFC 2119 constraints. May also be referred to as Agent Scripts (older name).
- brazil: Amazon's internal build system for Java/Kotlin (brazil-gradle), TypeScript/JS (npm-pretty-much), Python (brazil-python), Rust (CargoBrazil), Go, C/C++ (brazil-cmake). Use when working with Brazil packages, workspaces, dependencies, version sets, build commands, or encountering build errors and dependency conflicts.
- brazil-build-analyzer-skill: Diagnoses Brazil build failures by executing builds and parsing error output. Use when builds fail for packages, analyzing compilation errors, troubleshooting dependencies, or running tests in Brazil workspaces.
- builderhub-create: BuilderHub Create is where developers create new amazon internal packages or applications from templates. Use when asked to create a package, upload or promote a package, create an application, create application packages.
- crux-code-reviews: Amazon's internal code review system (CRUX) similar to GitHub PRs. Use when creating or revising CRs, addressing feedback, reviewing code, encountering cr CLI errors, or reviewing code changes at code.amazon.com/reviews/.
- deployment-fixer: Diagnose and fix failed, stuck, or blocked pipeline deployments, including CloudFormation deployment failures.
- fix-integration-test: Use when user has a test failure, wants to debug a test, or fix integration tests with ToD/Hydra URLs (tod.amazon.com/test_runs/*). Guides progressive investigation, log analysis, root cause identification, and fix verification.
- golden-path-recommendations: Golden Path Recommendations are opinionated guidance on proven tools, architecture patterns and best practices for applications. Use to get recommendations content, audit code against or create an implementation plan for your code base.
- improve-integration-test-coverage: Use when analyzing test coverage, improving test strategy, or recommending integration tests from CloudCover URLs (prod.ui.*.cloudcover.*/reports/*) or ToD URLs (tod.amazon.com/test_runs/*). Scores and prioritizes test candidates using 8-criteria rubric.
- skill-builder: Guide for creating and updating skills. Use when working with SKILL.md files, creating new skills, editing existing skills, or validating skill structure.
- wiki-edit: Amazon internal wiki edit operations using the raw tool, RustyAmazonWiki. When user asks to write, create, delete Amazon internal wiki pages. Or to edit attachments, comments, tags, or get page history.
</AvailableSkills>
